key_insight: Lattice reconstruction in TaCo2Te2 reshapes orbital hybridization, producing an electronic symmetry that differs from the atomic lattice and suppressing the material’s magnetic instability.
engineering_relevance: This suggests structural reconstruction can be used as a control knob to tune electronic anisotropy and switch correlated or magnetic behavior in layered quantum materials.
method: STM/STS to map surface topology and bias-dependent electronic contrast
nc-AFM to resolve the distorted Te lattice
ARPES plus orbital-projected DFT to connect Fermi-surface reconstruction with magnetism suppression
evidence_signal: The abstract reports complementary microscopy, spectroscopy, and DFT showing a square-like electronic symmetry, anisotropic Fermi surface, and a reconstructed nonmagnetic ground state.
watch_item: The STM contrast reflects energy-integrated local density of states, so it should not be interpreted as a direct one-to-one map of the atomic positions or a simple occupied/unoccupied-state reversal.
paper_title: Lattice Reconstruction and Orbital Hybridization Suppress Magnetism in TaCo$_2$Te$_2$
